title: NOC 94123 – Lumber graders and other wood processing inspectors and graders | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, as Canada’s forestry sector moves toward "Smart Forestry," Lumber Graders and Wood Processing Inspectors (NOC 94123) have become high-tech quality assurance specialists. These professionals ensure that every piece of timber—from structural beams for high-rise mass timber buildings to specialized pulp for eco-friendly packaging—meets rigorous national and international standards. With a "Good" labor market rating and a high volume of retirements expected through 2033, Canada is actively recruiting international graders who can work with both traditional physical inspection and modern automated scanning systems.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Quality Inspection: Examining lumber and wood products for defects (knots, cracks, rot) and sorting them according to established grading rules.
Standard Verification: Measuring wood products using calipers and gauges to ensure they meet precise thickness, width, and length specifications.
Testing: Using moisture meters and pressure gauges to verify that wood treatment or drying processes meet safety standards.
Reporting: Marking wood products with grade stamps and completing detailed inspection reports for plant management.
2026 Update: Calibrating and monitoring automated grading machines that use X-ray and 3D imaging to detect internal wood defects.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is required.
Experience: Several months of on-the-job training are provided; however, most employers require previous experience as a sawmill or wood processing labourer.
Certification: Lumber graders require a certificate from a recognized lumber grading agency (e.g., CLSAB - Canadian Lumber Standards Accreditation Board).
Skills: Strong attention to detail, good eyesight for defect detection, and the ability to work in fast-paced industrial environments.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates a "Balanced" market nationally, but "Shortages" in regional forestry hubs. With approximately 70% of openings coming from retirements, Canada is projected to need thousands of new inspectors and graders by 2033 to maintain its international quality certifications.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is driven by Canada's position as a global leader in sustainable wood exports:
British Columbia: Very Good prospects. As the heart of Canada's lumber industry, BC faces a chronic shortage of certified graders to support its massive export market.
Quebec: Strong demand. High demand in the Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean and Abitibi-Témiscamingue regions for quality control in pulp and paper mills.
New Brunswick: Good prospects. The forestry sector is a primary economic driver here, with consistent needs for inspectors in sawmills and treatment plants.
Ontario: Stable demand. Concentrated in Northern Ontario hubs like Thunder Bay, supporting both the lumber and board manufacturing sectors.
Why Work in Canada as a Lumber Grader/Inspector?
Critical Technical Role: You are the final authority on product quality, directly impacting the safety of construction and the value of exports.
Advanced QA Technology: Transition from manual grading to utilizing 2026-grade AI-assisted laser scanners and moisture-sensing technology.
High Job Stability: The global shift toward wood as a "green" construction material ensures long-term demand for certified inspectors.
Path to PR: Frequently featured in PNP In-Demand lists and eligible for the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
pathways: Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A premier 2026 pathway for graders to settle in regional forestry hubs with community-backed PR.
TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): A dedicated transition stream for 33,000 temporary residents. Wood processing inspectors are high-priority as "Essential Resource Workers."
BC PNP Skilled Worker: A reliable route for certified graders with a full-time job offer in British Columbia’s thriving forestry sector.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A direct PR route for those with job offers in the Atlantic provinces' wood processing sectors.
